title: BreakPoint: When Does Single-Step Perception from Generative Priors Break?
teaser: The dominant recipe for turning generative priors into perceivers is deterministic single-step regression — proven for depth and normals, asserted everywhere else. This project characterizes where it breaks, using layered amodal depth as a task ladder with controllable ambiguity: the further behind the visible surface, the more answers are correct.
abstract: A whole line of work converts pretrained diffusion models into dense predictors by fine-tuning them into deterministic single-step regressors — Lotus and follow-ups showed this wins for depth and normals, and generalist perceivers like GenCeption adopt single-step inference as a design principle. But that evidence comes entirely from unimodal tasks, where one answer is correct per pixel. Amodal geometry is different: the depth of the second surface behind a sofa is genuinely multi-modal — several walls, shelves, or nothing might be there — and a regressor must average over hypotheses while a sampler can commit to one. This project runs the controlled study that is missing: the same video diffusion backbone (WAN 2.1) is fine-tuned twice, once as a single-step regressor and once as a multi-step sampler, on a ladder of increasingly ambiguous tasks — visible depth, second-layer depth, K-th-layer depth — painted as prompt-switched answer videos. Synthetic scenes with multi-layer ground truth (rendered with the public LaRI pipeline on 3D-FRONT and ScanNet++ assets) make the true hypothesis set enumerable, so mode coverage, pixel accuracy, and calibration can be measured as a function of ambiguity, and the crossover point — where sampling starts to justify its cost — can be located quantitatively. External baselines: LaRI (deterministic layered ray intersections) and Amodal Depth Anything. The deliverable is practical guidance for the entire generative-prior perception line: when is one step enough, and when do you need to sample?
milestones: Literature review (single-step diffusion fine-tuning — Lotus, E2E-FT; layered/amodal geometry — LaRI, X-Ray, Amodal Depth Anything); set up the multi-layer ground-truth rendering pipeline (LaRI's public tooling on 3D-FRONT/ScanNet++ assets); pass the sanity gates on WAN 2.1 (1.3B) with LoRA: frozen-VAE round-trip of layered-depth videos, identity behavior at step zero, small-set overfit.
Regression arm: train the single-step prompt-switched model on visible and second-layer depth; benchmark visible depth against standard protocols and layered depth against LaRI.

Sampling arm: train the multi-step variant on the identical backbone and data; build the ambiguity-ladder evaluation — per-layer accuracy, best-of-N oracle scores, mode coverage against enumerable synthetic hypothesis sets, and calibration.

Analysis: locate and characterize the regression-to-sampling crossover as a function of layer index and scene ambiguity; qualitative transfer to real ScanNet++ scans; distill the findings into a decision rule for the paint-and-regress recipe.
(Stretch) Hybrid regimes: regress-then-refine, or an ambiguity-aware model that predicts when to sample — evaluated on the same ladder.

prerequisites.label: Advanced Deep Learning for Computer Vision: Visual Computing (ADL4CV)

references.label: Lotus: Diffusion-based Visual Foundation Model for High-quality Dense Prediction
Fine-Tuning Image-Conditional Diffusion Models is Easier than You Think
LaRI: Layered Ray Intersections for Single-view 3D Geometric Reasoning
X-Ray: A Sequential 3D Representation for Generation
Amodal Depth Anything: Amodal Depth Estimation in the Wild
GenCeption: Video Generation Models are General-Purpose Vision Learners
